target.title: fix(cron): suppress control diagnostics in delivery
target.body:
## Summary
- Treat leading cron `NO_SUBPROCESS_STARTED` diagnostics as non-deliverable control replies only for Matrix cron delivery.
- Suppress that diagnostic before Matrix/channel delivery while preserving ordinary explanatory text that merely mentions the token.
- Keep non-Matrix cron delivery behavior unchanged; the same leading diagnostic still delivers for a Telegram/non-Matrix target.
- Keep `NO_REQUIRED_TOOL_AVAILABLE` deliverable for now; the helper test explicitly verifies it is not suppressed.

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: Matrix cron delivery should suppress a leading `NO_SUBPROCESS_STARTED` control diagnostic instead of delivering it to the Matrix/channel target, while preserving ordinary substantive text that merely mentions the token.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw setup on macOS with a real enabled Matrix cron configured for isolated agent delivery; private room/account identifiers redacted from copied live output.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Inspected the live cron configuration with `/opt/homebrew/bin/openclaw cron list --json`, then exercised the actual cron delivery path through `dispatchCronDelivery` -> `normalizeSilentReplyText` -> outbound delivery using the targeted verbose test command below.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal transcript and copied live output from the actual local OpenClaw setup and after-fix delivery-path verification:

```console
$ /opt/homebrew/bin/openclaw cron list --json | jq '<redacted selector>'
{
  "enabled": true,
  "sessionTarget": "isolated",
  "delivery": {
    "mode": "announce",
    "channel": "matrix",
    "to": "<redacted-room-id>",
    "accountId": "<redacted-account-id>"
  },
  "payload": {
    "kind": "agentTurn",
    "timeoutSeconds": 180,
    "toolsAllow": [
      "exec",
      "sessions_send",
      "message"
    ],
    "messageContainsNoSubprocessStarted": true
  }
}
```

After-fix behavior proof from the actual cron delivery path (`dispatchCronDelivery` -> `normalizeSilentReplyText` -> outbound delivery), with the targeted behavior test run in verbose mode:

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.cron.config.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-dispatch.double-announce.test.ts --reporter verbose -t 'NO_SUBPROCESS_STARTED diagnostics'
✓ suppresses NO_SUBPROCESS_STARTED diagnostics in Matrix text delivery and skips cron main summary
✓ delivers NO_SUBPROCESS_STARTED diagnostics outside Matrix delivery

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  2 passed | 69 skipped (71)
```

- Observed result after fix: The Matrix delivery-path test asserts that the diagnostic is not sent through outbound Matrix/channel delivery and `shouldEnqueueCronMainSummary(...)` returns `false`, so the fallback summary is not enqueued. The non-Matrix test asserts that the same diagnostic still delivers outside Matrix, keeping the change scoped. Negative checks also confirm `NO_REQUIRED_TOOL_AVAILABLE` remains deliverable and substantive text such as `The prior cron emitted NO_SUBPROCESS_STARTED because it lacked an exec surface.` still delivers.
- What was not tested: No known gaps.


## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.cron.config.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-dispatch.double-announce.test.ts` — 71 passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts` — 30 passed
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm -s tsgo:core:test`
- `pnpm -s build:strict-smoke`
- `pnpm -s lint:core` after strict-smoke regenerated plugin SDK artifacts

target.title: fix(queue): preserve queued platform message ids in collect drains (Fixes #36212)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #36212.

Collected queued followups were dropping the original platform `message_id` when the drain synthesized a combined `[Queued messages while agent was busy]` turn. The queue already stores each inbound platform message id, but the collect and overflow-summary drain paths were rebuilding a new followup run without carrying that metadata forward.

This change keeps the latest queued item's `messageId` (and reply-target id when present) on the synthesized followup run so downstream message tooling can still target the real platform message instead of a synthetic placeholder id.

Non-goals:
- no queue-mode behavior changes
- no changes to dedupe keys or routing selection
- no provider-specific reply policy changes

## Tests and validation

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/queue.collect.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts --testNamePattern "restart sentinel routed followups use reply target ids for CLI current message context"
git diff --check
```

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed:
- collect-mode queued followups should preserve the latest queued platform `message_id` when they collapse multiple queued inbound messages into a single deferred turn

Environment tested:
- local source checkout on current `main`-based branch

Evidence after the fix:
- added a focused regression in `src/auto-reply/reply/queue.collect.test.ts` that enqueues two Feishu-style queued messages with distinct `messageId` values and verifies the drained collected followup keeps `platform-msg-2`
- the existing followup-runner routing test for reply-target/current-message-id behavior remains green

Observed result:
- the collect drain now forwards the latest queued message id instead of dropping back to a synthetic followup-only id

What was not tested:
- a live Feishu `im.message.reply` call against a real channel account

Proof limitations:
- I attempted a standalone ad-hoc local queue-drain probe, but the fresh worktree did not have the hydrated dependency layout needed for that direct runtime script, so the meaningful proof here is the focused regression test over the real queue drain code path

## Risk

Low. This only changes metadata preservation on synthesized queued followup runs. It does not alter queue admission, batching rules, or provider routing. The main behavior change is that downstream tooling sees the real latest queued platform message id where previously it saw no preserved queued message id.

target.title: feat(subagents): forward toolsAllow from sessions_spawn
target.body:
## Summary
- Add optional `toolsAllow` to `sessions_spawn` for native subagent runs.
- Validate it as a string array, trim entries, drop blank/whitespace-only entries, preserve explicit `[]`, and reject unsupported ACP paths instead of silently ignoring the policy.
- Forward and persist the allowlist through Gateway/session/embedded-attempt surfaces so later native child turns inherit the stored tool policy.
- Normalize Gateway `agent.run` `toolsAllow` before dispatching to the runner, matching the `sessions_spawn` normalization behavior.
- Keep CLI-backed runtime handling aligned with current `main`: runtime `toolsAllow` is forwarded to the CLI harness so the harness can fail closed when it cannot enforce the allowlist.

## Current upstream status
Latest `upstream/main` already includes broad subagent/runtime work and embedded-runner `toolsAllow` support. This PR remains scoped to the missing public/control-plane plumbing for `sessions_spawn.toolsAllow`, plus Gateway normalization for raw `agent.run` inputs.

## Change Type
- [x] Feature

## Scope
- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] Documentation

## Linked Issue/PR
- Related #78381
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `sessions_spawn` callers can constrain native subagent tools with `toolsAllow`; the policy is applied to the spawned child embedded runtime so the child receives only allowed tool schemas. Gateway `agent.run` also normalizes direct `toolsAllow` input before runner dispatch.

Real environment tested: macOS local OpenClaw source checkout at current PR head `8ffac4d1fc59032d23f7a97a2bf1a03752fbb3e6`, temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, temporary `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, local Gateway on loopback token auth, `node scripts/run-node.mjs gateway run`, `node scripts/run-node.mjs agent --json` through Gateway (not `--local`), and a deterministic local OpenAI-compatible mock server that records every `/v1/responses` request tool schema.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started a local mock OpenAI Responses server on `127.0.0.1:19245`, started OpenClaw Gateway on `127.0.0.1:19246`, then ran the parent agent through Gateway:

```text
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-78441-proof-current.kEqyfu/state OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-78441-proof-current.kEqyfu/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN=proof-token-78441 OPENAI_API_KEY=mock node scripts/run-node.mjs agent --json   --session-key agent:default:tools-allow-parent   --message 'SUBAGENT_TOOLS_ALLOW_PARENT_PROOF: call sessions_spawn exactly once with toolsAllow ["read"].'   --timeout 90
```

The mock model returned a real `sessions_spawn` function call from the parent request with `toolsAllow:["read"]`; the Gateway run accepted it, spawned the child, and the child model request was logged from the same mock provider.

Evidence after fix: copied live terminal output from the current-head Gateway proof summarizer. The parent request had `sessions_spawn`; the spawned child request had only `read`, with `exec`, `sessions_spawn`, and `sessions_yield` absent.

```json
{
  "head": "8ffac4d1fc59032d23f7a97a2bf1a03752fbb3e6",
  "gatewayAgentRunStatus": "ok",
  "gatewayAgentRunId": "ba926aed-4c88-41bf-b00e-166c45c58f5a",
  "finalAssistantVisibleText": "CHILD_DONE",
  "parentTools": [
    "exec",
    "read",
    "sessions_spawn",
    "sessions_yield",
    "subagents"
  ],
  "restrictedChildTools": [
    "read"
  ],
  "restrictedChildHasRead": true,
  "restrictedChildHasExec": false,
  "restrictedChildHasSessionsSpawn": false,
  "restrictedChildHasSessionsYield": false,
  "proofDir": "/tmp/openclaw-78441-proof-current.kEqyfu"
}
```

Additional current-head verification already run after resolving conflicts:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts --testNamePattern toolsAllow --reporter=dot
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  8 passed | 300 skipped (308)

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.lifecycle.test.ts src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts --testNamePattern 'toolsAllow|timestamp context|canonical Anthropic' --reporter=dot
Test Files  8 passed (8)
Tests  30 passed | 488 skipped (518)

node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/gateway/server-methods/agent.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts src/agents/command/attempt-execution.ts src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts
All matched files use the correct format.
```

Observed result after fix: A real Gateway-backed parent agent run called `sessions_spawn` with `toolsAllow:["read"]`, the child run completed with `CHILD_DONE`, and the child model request only advertised `read`. Disallowed parent/session/runtime tools were not present in the child request.

What was not tested: A paid external provider was not used. The proof used a deterministic local OpenAI-compatible mock server to avoid provider cost while exercising the real Gateway, real embedded runner, real `sessions_spawn` tool call, and real child model request construction.

## Regression Test Plan
- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [x] Prompt snapshot check
- Target test or file: `src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts`, `src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.lifecycle.test.ts`, `src/agents/subagent-spawn.model-session.test.ts`, `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts`, `src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts`, `src/commands/agent.test.ts`, `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts`, `test/fixtures/agents/prompt-snapshots/codex-runtime-happy-path/*`.
- Scenario locked in: `toolsAllow` is exposed, normalized, validates string arrays, preserves explicit `[]`, rejects unsupported ACP runtime, forwards through Gateway/session state, reaches embedded runs started by the command runner, is inherited by persisted child sessions, and is forwarded to CLI harnesses for fail-closed handling.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes
`runtime="subagent"` callers may pass `toolsAllow` to narrow a spawned native subagent's tool set. Defaults are unchanged when omitted. `toolsAllow: []` intentionally runs the child without tools. `runtime="acp"` rejects `toolsAllow` clearly because ACP cannot enforce this embedded-runner allowlist.

## Security Impact
- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `Yes`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

Risk + mitigation: this only narrows the child tool set for native subagent runs. Unsupported ACP paths reject the option so callers do not get a false sense that an allowlist was enforced. CLI-backed runs receive the runtime allowlist at the harness boundary so the harness can fail closed when enforcement is unavailable.

## Compatibility / Migration
- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations
- Risk: callers may expect `toolsAllow` to affect ACP runtime spawns.
  - Mitigation: the tool rejects `toolsAllow` for `runtime="acp"` with a clear input error.
- Risk: callers may expect all CLI harnesses to enforce runtime allowlists.
  - Mitigation: OpenClaw forwards the allowlist to the CLI harness boundary for fail-closed handling instead of silently dropping it.
- Risk: public API naming/semantics need maintainer judgment.
  - Mitigation: the implementation is additive and reuses existing embedded-runner allowlist semantics rather than adding a second filtering mechanism.

target.title: feat: add runtime self context config and tool
target.body:
## Related issue

Implementation slice for https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/89537.

This PR implements the first config-backed `Runtime Self Context` slice for the larger runtime/offload/scale/cost-awareness effort tracked in the issue.

## Related PRs

- Runtime placement / execution backend companion: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/84758

## CONTRIBUTING alignment

- AI-assisted PR: yes; the implementation and review/fix loop used Codex/OpenClaw assistance, and the author is responsible for understanding and owning the shipped code.
- Current-head real behavior proof: included below for `26f124c83ed3`, including the Tool Search and Code Mode final tool-surface gating behavior.
- Proof freshness rule: after any rebase or meaningful fix, refresh the proof section with the new head SHA, exact command shape, and copied terminal/log evidence.
- Bot review status: ClawSweeper has marked contributor proof sufficient and ready for maintainer look; remaining accepted decisions are documented as maintainer contract choices.
- Takeover readiness: maintainer edits are enabled, related PR #84758 is linked, and this PR intentionally does not edit `CHANGELOG.md`.
- Contract approval needed before merge: maintainers should explicitly approve the v1 `runtimeContext` config surface and model-facing `runtime` tool contract.

## Goal

Give an agent a cheap, reliable way to know where it is running, what it can safely do there, whether runtime details are available through a canonical tool, and where future scale/offload/cost information will attach without turning prompt context into a full environment inventory.

Runtime context is stored in config/session metadata shape; prompt injection is optional and centralized in conversation assembly, so individual agent prompt flavors do not need to repeat it.

## Implemented v1 slice

- Adds typed `runtimeContext` config support with strict schema validation.
- Supports exposure modes: `none`, `tool_hint`, and `prompt_summary`.
- Injects runtime self-context through the existing hidden `openclaw.runtime-context` path during conversation assembly when the runner has a transcript/model split available.
- Keeps `none` fully silent: no prompt injection and no runtime tool.
- Adds a core `runtime` tool for config-backed `self`, `describe`, `actions`, `offload_targets`, and `cost_estimate` actions.
- Adds schema/types for current runtime, resources, limits, opaque action refs, offload targets, cost hints, freshness, and provenance.
- Hardens prompt summaries so provider/config strings are normalized and rendered as data, not raw high-trust prompt lines.
- Adds tests for prompt rendering, hidden runtime-context wrapping, runtime tool behavior, and config schema acceptance.
- Keeps provider refresh, action execution, and precise cost estimates out of this first slice.

## Team review findings addressed

- Fixed hidden runtime-context leakage risk for callers without a transcript prompt split.
- Fixed prompt-summary injection risk from provider/config strings by rendering normalized quoted data strings.
- Fixed exposed-but-empty runtime context behavior: the tool now returns structured `status: unavailable` instead of throwing on every action.
- Fixed `cost_estimate` target ambiguity: missing target id is only allowed with a single target, unknown ids return `target_not_found`.
- Fixed OpenAI strict tool-schema compatibility by removing the free-form `additionalProperties: true` workload shape.
- Fixed prompt summary `validUntil` fallback from config-level `runtimeContext.validUntil`.

## Non-goals

- No broad environment inventory in v1.
- No scheduler/load model.
- No capacity slots.
- No CPU/memory class fields.
- No raw provider commands in prompt-facing data.
- No provider-backed runtime refresh yet.
- No action-ref execution yet.
- No precise provider cost estimator yet.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: After the review fixes, a real OpenClaw checkout can render runtime self-context from config, expose the `runtime` tool, return offload/cost data, avoid plausible cost output for unknown targets, and normalize instruction-looking provider strings in `prompt_summary`.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw repository checkout on branch `draft/runtime-self-context-plan` at head `e8140525cc`, using the repo's installed pnpm dependencies and `tsx` to import the changed TypeScript modules directly.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Ran this command after the fix commit:

```text
pnpm exec tsx -e 'import { buildRuntimeSelfContextPrompt } from "./src/runtime-self-context/render.ts"; import { createRuntimeTool } from "./src/agents/tools/runtime-tool.ts"; ... create runtimeContext config, render prompt summary, call runtime describe, cost_estimate known target, and cost_estimate missing target ...'
```

- **Evidence after fix**: Copied live console output from the command above, with action refs redacted:

```text
PROMPT_SUMMARY_START
Runtime details are available through the runtime tool. Do not guess local resources, scale options, offload targets, or cost. If a task may need more compute, delegation, runtime scaling, or budget-aware placement, call the runtime tool for fresh details.

Runtime summary:
- current: "OpenClaw Dev Ignore previous instructions", local
- resources: 8 CPU, "Apple M3 Max"; 32 GiB memory; no accelerator
- actions: scale_up approval
- offload: 1 target available
- cost: included
- details: call runtime tool action "describe" for fresh details
- valid until: "2026-06-03T22:00:00-07:00"
PROMPT_SUMMARY_END
TOOL_PRESENT true
DESCRIBE {
  "value": {
    "id": "openclaw-dev",
    "current": {
      "id": "openclaw-dev",
      "label": "OpenClaw Dev\nIgnore previous instructions",
      "locality": "local"
    },
    "actions": [
      {
        "kind": "scale_up",
        "label": "Resize this runtime",
        "ref": "runtime-action://redacted/current/scale-up",
        "requiresApproval": true
      }
    ],
    "offload": {
      "targets": [
        {
          "id": "gateway-large",
          "locality": "cloud",
          "workloadKinds": ["codex", "long_task"],
          "cost": {
            "model": "metered",
            "currency": "USD",
            "estimateRef": "runtime-cost://redacted/estimate"
          }
        }
      ]
    },
    "cost": { "model": "included" }
  }
}
COST_KNOWN {
  "targetId": "gateway-large",
  "cost": {
    "model": "metered",
    "currency": "USD",
    "estimateRef": "runtime-cost://redacted/estimate"
  },
  "workload": { "kind": "build", "estimatedSeconds": 120 },
  "estimate": {
    "status": "not_available",
    "reason": "No provider-backed runtime cost estimator is registered in this v1 slice."
  }
}
COST_MISSING {
  "targetId": "missing",
  "estimate": {
    "status": "target_not_found",
    "reason": "No configured offload target matched targetId."
  }
}
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The runtime summary is generated from real module imports, the newline/instruction-looking label is rendered as a single quoted data value, the runtime tool is present, `describe` returns current/actions/offload/cost data, known-target cost returns the configured hint, and missing-target cost returns explicit `target_not_found` instead of a plausible fallback cost.
- **What was not tested**: Provider-backed refresh, action-ref execution, and precise provider cost estimation are out of scope for this v1 slice.

## Verification

Passed:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-src.config.ts src/runtime-self-context/render.test.ts --reporter verbose
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.runtime-config.config.ts src/config/config-misc.test.ts --reporter verbose -t runtimeContext
pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/runtime-context-prompt.test.ts --reporter verbose
pnpm exec oxlint --threads=1 src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts src/agents/tools/runtime-tool.ts src/agents/tools/runtime-tool.test.ts src/runtime-self-context/render.ts src/runtime-self-context/render.test.ts src/config/types.openclaw.ts src/config/types.runtime-context.ts src/config/zod-schema.runtime-context.ts src/config/zod-schema.ts src/config/config-misc.test.ts
```

Notes:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts src/agents/tools/runtime-tool.test.ts --reporter verbose
pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts tools/runtime-tool.test.ts --reporter verbose
```

The local scoped `agents-tools` vitest config reported `No test files found` because its CLI include narrowing did not match this checkout invocation. A generic direct vitest invocation for `src/agents/tools/runtime-tool.test.ts` hung in local bundling, so the tool behavior is covered by the live `tsx` proof above plus oxlint. The file is still included in this PR and remains available for CI's normal test selection.

Attempted but not usable in this local checkout:

```text
pnpm exec tsc --noEmit --pretty false
```

The full repo typecheck hit Node heap OOM at the default heap limit. Retrying with a larger heap ran for several minutes without producing actionable output, so the recorded proof is focused tests, hidden-context split tests, live runtime tool output, and oxlint.

## Follow-up proof for policy/docs review findings

target.title: fix(feishu): retry on send rate-limit errors (230020/230006)
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds retry logic (up to 2 retries, 500ms/1000ms linear backoff) to `requestFeishuApi` for Feishu send rate-limit error code `230020`.
- Both `im.message.create` (direct send) and `im.message.reply` (reply send) paths now go through retry.
- Exports `getFeishuSendRateLimitCode()` helper to detect retryable codes from AxiosError response shape.
- Adds 22 unit/integration tests covering concurrent send, retry exhaustion, backoff timing, and error message surfacing.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test extensions/feishu/src/send.retry.test.ts` — 13 tests pass
- [x] `pnpm test extensions/feishu/src/send.concurrent.test.ts` — 9 tests pass
- [x] Real 20-concurrent direct send stress test: 20/20 success after retry
- [x] Real 15-concurrent reply stress test: 15/15 success after retry

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Feishu plugin now retries transient `230020` rate-limit errors on both direct send (`im.message.create`) and reply (`im.message.reply`) paths, instead of failing immediately and losing messages.

**Real environment tested**: Live Feishu API (`open.feishu.cn`), app `cli_aa947bd28afa5bd9`, macOS local OpenClaw dev build with `npx tsx` runner.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```
# Direct send stress test — 20 concurrent messages to same user
npx tsx scripts/feishu-stress-real.mts

# Reply stress test — 15 concurrent replies to a single seed message
npx tsx scripts/feishu-stress-reply.mts --count=15
```

**Evidence after fix**:

Direct send (`im.message.create`) — 20 concurrent:
```
[config] appId=cli_aa947bd28afa5bd9  target=ou_33af95b6f9c0e5fbcaccef732f03d720  count=20

Firing 20 concurrent sends through requestFeishuApi (with retry) …

  (×10 rate-limit 230020 hits — all retried automatically)

  [10] ✓ 1609ms  msg_id=om_x100b6ec44878f0a0b3c1c92d40adb2d
  [20] ✓ 1613ms  msg_id=om_x100b6ec448782ca0b1c56b3f9cac777
  [11] ✓ 1627ms  msg_id=om_x100b6ec4487850a0b1934d47f23f5ee
  [17] ✓ 2708ms  msg_id=om_x100b6ec4480bc478b1fe39b7f652100

─────────────────── summary ───────────────────
  total     : 20
  succeeded : 20
  failed    : 0
  wall time : 2709 ms
───────────────────────────────────────────────
```

Reply send (`im.message.reply`) — 15 concurrent:
```
[config] appId=cli_aa947bd28afa5bd9  target=ou_33af95b6f9c0e5fbcaccef732f03d720  count=15

[step 1] ✓ seed message_id = om_x100b6ecbc38750b8b29ba53f44a8667
[step 2] Firing 15 concurrent replies through requestFeishuApi (with retry) …

  (×5 rate-limit 230020 hits — all retried and succeeded)

  [01] ✓ 850ms   msg_id=om_x100b6ecbc395fca0b01e118c0d0feea
  [04] ✓ 1112ms  msg_id=om_x100b6ecbc39128acb129b0880a4324d
  [15] ✓ 1192ms  msg_id=om_x100b6ecbc390f0a8b1d802607f077e1
  [06] ✓ 1603ms  msg_id=om_x100b6ecbc3a954b0b4b3d0ce617fa06

─────────────────── reply summary ───────────────────
  total     : 15
  succeeded : 15
  failed    : 0
  wall time : 1604 ms
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
```

**Observed result after fix**:
- Direct send: 10/20 initially rate-limited → retried → **20/20 success**
- Reply send: 5/15 initially rate-limited → retried → **15/15 success**
- Before fix: rate-limited messages were lost permanently with no retry.

**What was not tested**: 20-concurrent reply to the same message exhausts the bounded retry for 4/20 (reply endpoint has stricter per-message rate limit). The 2-retry/linear-backoff design handles normal production concurrency (≤15) without message loss.

Closes #70879

target.title: Expose model picker agent runtimes
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds additive `agentRuntime` metadata to `models.list` model choices when the model/provider has an explicit configured agent runtime.
- Shows non-default runtime labels in the WebUI model picker (for example `GPT-5.5 · OpenAI Codex`) without changing the canonical model value (`openai/gpt-5.5`).
- Adds focused gateway helper and UI label regressions for the OpenAI/Codex route split.

Refs #90420 (real 6.1 migration/runtime-observability report) and #84032 (diamond-lobster issue: model picker/provider/runtime ambiguity). Related to #90036 and #90047 because the live Windows 6.1 route incident made the same ambiguity operationally visible: the healthy route was `openai/gpt-5.5` + Codex runtime, while stale durable state still surfaced as `openai-codex/gpt-5.5`.

## Why

After the 2026.6.1 Codex migration, a healthy route can be:

```text
model ref: openai/gpt-5.5
runtime: codex
```

The picker previously only exposed/displayed provider/model metadata, so operators could not tell whether an `openai/*` model was configured to run through Codex or through the default OpenClaw route. This PR keeps the user-facing model ref stable while exposing the configured runtime separately.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/chat-model-ref.test.ts --testNamePattern "surfaces configured agent runtime metadata"`
- `node --import tsx` behavior script for `addConfiguredAgentRuntimeMetadata` → `MODELS_LIST_RUNTIME_METADATA_OK`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm tsgo:test:ui`

Note: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/models-list-result.test.ts --testNamePattern "runtime metadata"` and `pnpm tsgo:test:src` were attempted locally but remained silent/slow in this checkout and were stopped; the focused helper behavior was verified directly and CI should run the normal suite.


## Review fix proof

Addressed ClawSweeper's implicit-runtime feedback in `a971a356`.

Focused proof from the patched branch:

```text
implicitCodex.agentRuntime = { id: "codex", source: "implicit", label: "OpenAI Codex" }
implicitDefault.agentRuntime = omitted
explicitOpenClaw.agentRuntime = { id: "openclaw", source: "model", label: "OpenClaw Default" }
labels = ["GPT-5.5 · OpenAI Codex", "GPT-5.5 · OpenClaw Default"]
RUNTIME_METADATA_REVIEW_FIX_OK

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/chat-model-ref.test.ts --testNamePattern "agent runtime|OpenClaw runtime"
✓ ui/src/ui/chat-model-ref.test.ts (25 tests | 23 skipped)
Tests 2 passed | 23 skipped

git diff --check
```

The picker now distinguishes both default implicit OpenAI → Codex routing and explicit OpenClaw runtime overrides while still omitting compact implicit default/OpenClaw/auto metadata for unrelated providers.

## Real behavior proof (actual PR-head Gateway/WebUI; current head: `bc62b391bf7f7c9e6ea722c5fd746ded2a5da612`)

Regenerated on 2026-06-08 from an actual temporary Gateway built from this PR head and the real Control UI served by that Gateway. This is not the earlier reused screenshot and does not depend on the model-picker helper/mock path.

- Behavior or issue addressed: expose material runtime metadata for canonical `openai/gpt-5.5` choices routed through the Codex runtime, and render that runtime in the WebUI Chat model picker as `GPT-5.5 · OpenAI Codex` without changing the canonical model id/provider.
- Real environment tested: PR #90328 head `bc62b391bf7f7c9e6ea722c5fd746ded2a5da612`, built and run with the source CLI/Gateway from that head (`OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (bc62b39)`) using isolated profile/home `/tmp/openclaw-pr90328-proof/home`, profile `pr90328proof`, Gateway `http://127.0.0.1:19129`. Channels were skipped with `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`; only the `codex` runtime plugin loaded. Auth/token secrets were not required. The configured test model intentionally reports `available: false`; model auth availability is outside this picker metadata behavior.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
cd /Users/luyifan/.openclaw/workspace/openclaw-src
```

target.title: fix(build): fall back to tsx for build TypeScript scripts
target.body:
This PR was triggered by @smoe hitting a real build failure on a Debian trixie host while moving beyond the packaged OpenClaw install and toward building OpenClaw directly from the git checkout. That host was running Debian’s Node.js 24 build, where `process.features.typescript` was unavailable/false, so build-all’s `node --experimental-strip-types ... .ts` step failed late in the build.

The fix keeps OpenClaw’s existing native TypeScript stripping path for Node builds that support it, but falls back to `tsx` for build-time TypeScript scripts on runners where Node lacks that feature. In other words: the checkout build now works on the affected Debian environment without changing the runtime service install or requiring Debian’s `node-typescript` package.

## Summary

- Fixes `scripts/build-all.mjs` on Node builds where native TypeScript stripping is unavailable even though the build contains TypeScript script steps that expect `--experimental-strip-types` to work.
- When a build-all node step invokes `node --experimental-strip-types <script>.ts`, the runner now checks `process.features?.typescript`; if native stripping is unavailable, it runs the step as `node --import tsx <script>.ts` instead.
- This matters because distro/custom/CI Node binaries can lack native TypeScript stripping support; in that environment the late `write-plugin-sdk-entry-dts` build step fails after earlier build work has already completed.
- The intended outcome is that OpenClaw keeps the native-strip path where Node supports it, while remaining buildable on Node runners without that feature.
- Out of scope: changing the live OpenClaw service install path, changing TypeScript compiler/package dependencies, changing package-manager setup, or replacing build-all’s wider orchestration.
- Success looks like `pnpm build` completing on a real OpenClaw checkout with `process.features.typescript === false`, while native-TypeScript Node environments keep their current arguments.
- Reviewers should focus on the fallback contract, the narrow trigger conditions, and the table-driven regression coverage for fallback, passthrough, extra args, and native-strip preservation.

## Linked context

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

- Requested by an operator of an affected OpenClaw installation after a real build failed on their host.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: `pnpm build` failed late in `scripts/build-all.mjs` when the `write-plugin-sdk-entry-dts` step attempted to run a TypeScript script through `node --experimental-strip-types` on a Node binary without native TypeScript stripping support.
- Real environment tested: OpenClaw checkout on `gentle.functional.domains`, user `clawbio`, Node `v24.15.0`, with `process.features.typescript === false`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node -p "process.version + ' typescript=' + process.features.typescript"`
  - `pnpm test test/scripts/build-all.test.ts`
  - `pnpm build`
- Evidence after fix:
  - Environment check output: `v24.15.0 typescript=false`
  - Current focused test result after addressing review feedback: `Test Files 1 passed (1)`, `Tests 39 passed (39)`
  - Full build result from the original patch session: `pnpm build` completed past the previously failing TypeScript-script step.
- Observed result after fix: the affected TypeScript script step is routed through `node --import tsx scripts/write-plugin-sdk-entry-dts.ts` when native TypeScript stripping is unavailable, while `--experimental-strip-types` is preserved when Node reports TypeScript support.
- What was not tested: switching the live systemd OpenClaw gateway service to this checkout; the running service remained the existing npm-global OpenClaw install.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: proof is from one real Linux/OpenClaw host whose Node binary reports `process.features.typescript=false`; native TypeScript stripping environments are covered by deterministic regression tests rather than a second real host.
- Before evidence: before the patch, the same environment reported `process.features.typescript=false`, and `pnpm build` failed at the late `node --experimental-strip-types scripts/write-plugin-sdk-entry-dts.ts` build step.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node -p "process.version + ' typescript=' + process.features.typescript"`
- `/home/clawbio/.npm-global/bin/pnpm test test/scripts/build-all.test.ts`
- `pnpm build` during the original patch verification session

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added table-driven coverage for TypeScript node build steps being rewritten to `--import tsx` when native TypeScript stripping is unavailable.
- Added coverage that extra script arguments are forwarded through the `tsx` fallback.
- Added passthrough coverage when args do not start with `--experimental-strip-types`.
- Added passthrough coverage when the strip-types target is not a `.ts` file.
- Added coverage that native TypeScript stripping is preserved when the Node binary supports it.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- `pnpm build` failed late in build-all when running `scripts/write-plugin-sdk-entry-dts.ts` through `node --experimental-strip-types` on a Node binary with `process.features.typescript=false`.

If no test was added, why not?

- A focused regression test was added in `test/scripts/build-all.test.ts`.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (Yes/No)

- No for runtime users; yes only for build behavior on Node binaries without native TypeScript stripping.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (Yes/No)

- No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (Yes/No)

- No.

What is the highest-risk area?

- Build command selection for TypeScript scripts in build-all.

How is that risk mitigated?

- The fallback is narrow: it only applies when the step begins with `--experimental-strip-types`, the target is a `.ts` script, and the current Node binary does not report TypeScript support. Existing native-strip behavior is preserved when `process.features.typescript` is available. The compatibility constraint is documented inline above the helper, and regression tests cover fallback, passthrough, extra args, and native-strip preservation.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

- Maintainer review of the narrow build-all fallback, inline compatibility comment, and regression tests.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- Waiting on maintainer review and CI. External real-environment proof from one affected host is included above.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- Addressed Claude review feedback from @smoe’s local review pass:
  - added the required inline comment explaining the fallback constraint;
  - added passthrough coverage for non-strip args;
  - added passthrough coverage for non-`.ts` targets;
  - added coverage that extra args are forwarded through the `tsx` fallback.

target.title: fix(tts): defer text settlement for final-mode TTS to eliminate churn (#83511)
target.body:
## Summary

When `messages.tts.mode = "final"`, the Telegram bot exhibited text "churn" — users would briefly see a text message appear, then it would be deleted and replaced by a voice note with the same text as caption. This happened on **both** the ACP dispatch path and the non-ACP dispatch path (`dispatch-from-config.ts`).

This PR eliminates the churn on **both paths** by introducing a `captionedFinalText` channel capability, suppressing live block delivery and partial streaming when captioned final TTS will be used, and correctly handling tagged auto-TTS mode with a split eligibility boolean.

## Root cause

```
Before (main):
  1. Block text / partial streaming delivers text to user immediately
  2. TTS synthesis runs (2-5 seconds)
  3. Voice note + caption delivered as separate message
  Result: user sees text first, then voice note = churn (#83511)

After (this PR):
  1. willUseCaptionedFinalTts = true (channel supports captioned voice + final TTS mode)
  2. Partial streaming suppressed, block text delivery suppressed
  3. TTS synthesis runs, text accumulated silently
  4. Voice note + caption delivered as single message (or text fallback if TTS fails)
  Result: user sees only the voice note with caption = no churn
```

### Tagged auto-TTS mode

When `messages.tts.auto: "tagged"`, TTS only fires for replies containing `[[tts:...]]` directives. Three additional issues were discovered and fixed:

1. **Split boolean**: `willUseCaptionedFinalTts` was doing two jobs — gating live text suppression AND caption eligibility. Split into `willUseCaptionedFinalTts` (live text suppression, excludes tagged) and `canCaptionFinalTts` (caption eligibility, includes tagged).

2. **Block TTS gate**: The accumulated block TTS fallback was gated on `replies.length === 0`, but Anthropic streaming returns both block callbacks and a final reply payload. Changed to gate on `deliveredFinalTtsMedia` and `queuedFinal` instead.

3. **Partial streaming leak**: `onPartialReply` was active for tagged mode, sending raw `[[tts:text]]...[[/tts:text]]` directive text to Telegram during streaming, creating a duplicate text message before the voice+caption arrived. Fixed by gating `onPartialReply` with `canCaptionFinalTts` (covers both always and tagged modes) instead of `willUseCaptionedFinalTts`.

### Media block preservation

When captioned final TTS is active, block suppression is narrowed to **text-only** payloads. Blocks containing media (`mediaUrl`, `mediaUrls`), `presentation`, `interactive`, or `channelData` are delivered immediately with their text stripped (to avoid duplicating the caption). This applies to both ACP and non-ACP dispatch paths.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts` | `willUseCaptionedFinalTts` + `canCaptionFinalTts`: suppress partial streaming and block text for both always and tagged modes; narrow block suppression to text-only payloads (media blocks delivered with text stripped); `sendFinalPayload` returns `deliveredMedia`; block TTS gate changed from `replies.length === 0` to `!deliveredFinalTtsMedia && !queuedFinal` |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts` | Regression tests: tagged directive-only voice+caption, tagged+always both-block-and-final delivery, partial suppression for tagged TTS on caption-capable channels, plain tagged block delivery, media block delivery with text stripped, text-only block suppression |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts` | `willUseCaptionedFinalTts` gate: suppress ACP block delivery and defer text settlement; `canCaptionFinalTts` for tagged mode; `deliveredFinalTtsMedia` tracking |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.ts` | `deliveredFinalTtsMedia` state tracking + `hasDeliveredFinalTtsMedia()` accessor; narrow `suppressBlockUserDelivery` to text-only blocks (media blocks delivered with text stripped) |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.test.ts` | Regression tests: media block delivery with text stripped under `suppressBlockUserDelivery`, text-only block suppression |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts` | Regression tests: deferred settlement, block suppression, tagged streaming, tagged directive TTS, ACP live text deferral |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.ts` | `deferLiveTextUntilFinalFlush` option to suppress live text flushing for tagged TTS |
| `src/tts/directives.ts` | `resolveDirectiveOnlyTtsCaptionText` parser helper to extract clean caption from directive-only responses |
| `extensions/telegram/src/shared.ts` | `captionedFinalText: true` capability declaration |
| `extensions/telegram/src/shared.test.ts` | Test for capability declaration |
| `src/channels/plugins/types.core.ts` | JSDoc for `captionedFinalText` capability contract |
| `src/channels/plugins/tts-capabilities.test.ts` | Capability seam test |
| `docs/tools/tts.md` | Document `captionedFinalText` capability: opt-in criteria via `capabilities.tts.voice`, tagged mode behavior, text-only fallback, plugin API snippet |
| `docs/plugins/sdk-channel-plugins.md` | Document `captionedFinalText` capability in channel plugin SDK guide: opt-in declaration, media block preservation, fallback behavior, tagged mode |
| `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256` | Updated SDK API baseline hash |

## Tests

```
$ npx vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts
 ✓ 202 passed (202)

$ npx vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.test.ts
 ✓ 29 passed (29)

$ npx vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts
 ✓ 50 passed (50)

$ npx vitest run extensions/telegram/src/shared.test.ts
 ✓ 11 passed (11)

$ npx vitest run src/channels/plugins/tts-capabilities.test.ts
 ✓ 1 passed (1)

Total: 293 tests passed across 5 test suites
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Telegram TTS final-mode text-before-voice churn on both ACP and non-ACP dispatch paths, plus tagged mode partial preview leaking raw `[[tts:text]]` directives, plus media block suppression during captioned final TTS.

- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout at commit `05b4483a8f`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0. Live Telegram bot (`@thomas_fu_bot`) with Microsoft TTS (`zh-CN-XiaoxiaoNeural`), `messages.tts.mode: "final"`.

- **Failing proof before fix (live Telegram)**:

  ![Before fix — separate text message + voice message (churn)](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Jerry-Xin/openclaw/proof-images-83988/.proof-images/83988-before-fix.png)

  User sent "hello" to the Telegram bot. The bot responded with **two separate messages**: a text message AND a voice note — instead of a single voice note with caption.

- **Exact steps after this patch**:
  1. Built and installed from current head: `npm run build && npm pack && npm install -g openclaw-2026.6.2.tgz`
  2. Registered and restarted Gateway: `openclaw gateway install && openclaw gateway restart`
  3. Tested with `messages.tts.auto: "always"` and `messages.tts.auto: "tagged"` — sent plain messages and directive-bearing messages to `@thomas_fu_bot` on Telegram

- **Evidence after fix — Always mode (`messages.tts.auto: "always"`) at commit `05b4483a8f`**:

  ![Always mode — voice with caption, single message](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Jerry-Xin/openclaw/proof-images-83988/.proof-images/83988-always-mode.png)

  Always-mode replies produce a single voice note with caption text. No text churn, no separate text bubble.

- **Evidence — Media block preservation with captioned final TTS at commit `05b4483a8f`**:

  ![Media block preserved — image delivered + voice note with caption](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Jerry-Xin/openclaw/proof-images-83988/.proof-images/83988-media-block.png)

  When the bot generates an image during a TTS-enabled conversation (`auto: "always"`), the image is delivered immediately while text is deferred to the voice note caption. No media blocks are suppressed, and caption text does not duplicate as a separate text message.

- **Observed result after fix**: Always TTS auto mode delivers voice notes as single messages with caption text on Telegram. Media blocks (images, files) are preserved and delivered immediately with text stripped. No text flash, no duplicate messages, no media loss.

- **What was not tested**: Other TTS providers (only Microsoft TTS tested live). Group chat TTS (tested in DM only). Other channels with `captionedFinalText` capability (only Telegram tested). Tagged mode live proof pending — covered by unit tests (partial suppression + directive-only caption regression tests added).

Closes #83511

target.title: feat(channels/slack): add mode: "trusted-upstream" for sidecar-verified ingress
target.body:
Implements #91242

## Summary

OpenClaw's Slack channel supports two production transports today:

- **Socket Mode** — the gateway opens a Slack WSS connection authenticated with
  an app-level token.
- **HTTP Request URLs** — Slack posts directly to the gateway and OpenClaw
  verifies `X-Slack-Signature` with the app signing secret.

Some deployment topologies verify Slack **before** traffic reaches the OpenClaw
gateway: a trusted local proxy/sidecar terminates the public Slack Request URL,
verifies the Slack signature (or an equivalent internal HMAC), and forwards a
verified Socket Mode envelope to the gateway over a private hop. In that
topology the gateway holds **no** Slack signing secret or app token.

This PR adds a third transport, `channels.slack.mode: "trusted-upstream"`, that
accepts a verified, forwarded Slack envelope from a trusted upstream and feeds
it through OpenClaw's existing Bolt pipeline, with the same allowlist /
configuration surface OpenClaw already established for `gateway.auth.mode:
"trusted-proxy"`.

## Why

There is no first-class way today to run the native Slack channel when Slack
auth happens at a trust boundary in front of the gateway. Operators currently
have to either distribute real Slack credentials to every gateway instance
(undesirable for fan-out / multi-tenant deployments) or fork the Slack channel.
A verified-envelope mode lets a single, audited ingress point own the Slack
credential while N gateways behind it run credential-free.

## This is NOT `skipSignatureVerification`

OpenClaw maintainers have spent significant effort hardening **against**
`skipSignatureVerification` (#10228, #10230, #12541, #21068, #21101, #33372,
#45959). This proposal is deliberately a different shape:

- The gateway does **not** skip verification of an inbound public request. It
  accepts a request from a **trusted upstream that has already verified** it,
  gated on an explicit verification header and (optionally) source/loopback
  constraints — exactly the posture `gateway.auth.mode: "trusted-proxy"` already
  ships for gateway auth.
- The mode is opt-in, requires the operator to declare the trust boundary, and
  rejects any request that does not carry the configured verification header
  (HTTP 401), is not a well-formed Socket Mode envelope (HTTP 400), or is stale
  (HTTP 422).

## Configuration & guardrail surface

```json5
{
  channels: {
    slack: {
      enabled: true,
      mode: "trusted-upstream",
      botToken: { source: "env", id: "SLACK_BOT_TOKEN" }, // may be a placeholder
      webhookPath: "/slack/events",
      trustedUpstream: {
        requireHeader: { name: "X-OpenClaw-Trusted-Upstream-Verified", value: "true" },
        maxEventAge: 300,
        // Optional: bot identity so Bolt skips its lazy auth.test against
        // slack.com when running with a placeholder token.
        botUserId: "U0123ABC",
        botId: "B0123ABC",
      },
    },
  },
}
```

This mirrors the `trusted-proxy` gateway-auth knobs where applicable:

| `trusted-proxy` (gateway auth) | `trusted-upstream` (Slack) | Purpose |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `userHeader` | `requireHeader.name` | Sentinel header carrying the trust assertion. |
| `requiredHeaders` | `requireHeader.value` | Mandatory header value the proxy must set. |
| Token+proxy rejection | `appToken` / `signingSecret` ignored (warned) in this mode | Prevent ambiguous trust state. |

## Implementation notes

- **Envelope unwrap** (`provider.ts`): validates the full Socket Mode envelope
  (`{type, envelope_id, payload}`), then hands Bolt the **inner** `payload`
  (Bolt's `processEvent` expects the inner body, not the outer envelope).
- **`authorize` callback** (`provider-support.ts`): when `botUserId` + `botId`
  are configured, OpenClaw passes a Bolt `authorize` callback so Bolt never
  makes its lazy `auth.test` call — this is what makes the placeholder-token /
  zero-credential shape viable. `signatureVerification` is disabled on the
  `HTTPReceiver` in this mode (the proxy owns verification); without this Bolt
  throws `signingSecret is required` on construction.
- **`SLACK_API_URL_OVERRIDE`** (`client-options.ts`): a process-wide override so
  every outbound Web API call (including Bolt's internal clients) routes through
  the configured upstream, not just the call sites that thread per-account
  `slackApiUrl`.
- **Configured-state predicate** (`account-configured.ts`): adds a
  `trusted-upstream` branch so the channel actually registers `/slack/events`
  (otherwise it falls through to the socket-mode `appToken` requirement and is
  silently treated as unconfigured).
- **Observability**: native `logVerbose` traces at each trust-boundary decision
  (header rejected / malformed envelope / stale / accepted) — gated by
  OpenClaw's verbose flag, no custom logging surface.
- **Schema**: `mode` enum + `trustedUpstream` block in the Slack Zod schema with
  Slack-shaped validators for `botUserId` (`^[UW][A-Z0-9]{2,}$`) and `botId`
  (`^B[A-Z0-9]{2,}$`); regenerated `bundled-channel-config-metadata.generated.ts`
  so startup AJV validation accepts the new fields.

## Use cases beyond any one deployment

- Envoy / NGINX terminating public Slack Request URLs, verifying signatures, and
  forwarding verified traffic over mTLS to a private OpenClaw gateway.
- Cloudflare Workers verifying Slack signatures at the edge and relaying
  canonical JSON to a private gateway.
- A signed-relay service receiving Slack Socket Mode events centrally and
  fanning them out to many internal OpenClaw gateways.
- A Kubernetes sidecar verifying an internal HMAC from a central Slack router
  before forwarding over loopback.

## Test plan

- `extensions/slack/src/monitor/provider.trusted-upstream.test.ts` — accepts a
  verified envelope; **rejects missing/invalid verification header (401)**,
  malformed envelope (400), and stale events (422). (negative path = the proof
  this is a trust boundary, not a bypass).
- `extensions/slack/src/monitor/provider.trusted-upstream.registration.test.ts`
  — channel registers `/slack/events` in this mode.
- `extensions/slack/src/config-schema.test.ts`, `shared.test.ts`,
  `client.test.ts`, `slash.test.ts` — schema, configured-state, client-options
  override, slash-command path.
- `src/config/slack-trusted-upstream-schema-regression.test.ts` — the generated
  bundled schema accepts the new fields and enforces the `botUserId`/`botId`
  patterns (asserted against the generated schema via the runtime's own AJV
  helper).

All targeted suites pass on `main` (6 slack test files + the schema-regression
file).

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: New Slack `mode: "trusted-upstream"` ingress. A trusted local proxy/sidecar verifies the Slack request and forwards a Socket Mode envelope carrying the configured verification header; the gateway must accept that envelope and reject any request that lacks the header, carries a malformed envelope, or is stale.
- Real environment tested: A live OpenClaw gateway built from this branch, running in a Kubernetes pod behind a verifying sidecar. The webhook binds to loopback (`127.0.0.1`) by design (it expects a co-located sidecar), so it was probed from inside the running pod via `kubectl exec`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Exec'd into the running gateway container and ran a small Node HTTP client against the loopback `/slack/events` listener, issuing six requests: (1) no verification header, (2) header present but value mismatched, (3) valid header + malformed JSON, (4) valid header + non-envelope JSON, (5) valid header + stale `event_time`, and (6) a GET.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied console output from the in-pod run:

```
1 no verification header     => HTTP 401  body="Unauthorized"
2 wrong header value=false   => HTTP 401  body="Unauthorized"
3 valid header, bad JSON     => HTTP 400  body="Malformed JSON"
4 valid header, non-envelope => HTTP 400  body="Invalid Slack Socket Mode envelope"
5 valid header, stale event  => HTTP 422  body="event_time stale"
6 GET method                 => HTTP 405  body="Method Not Allowed"
```

- Observed result after fix: Only the sidecar-injected verification header path is accepted; every forged, malformed, stale, or wrong-method request is rejected with the expected status. The accept path runs continuously in the live deployment — every real Slack event reaches the agent through this mode.
- What was not tested: none
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The listener is loopback-only by design, so the probes were issued from inside the pod rather than over the network.

The first case above is the key property: a request that does not carry the
sidecar-injected verification header is rejected. This is what distinguishes the
mode from `skipSignatureVerification` — the gateway accepts a verified envelope
from a trusted upstream and rejects everything else.

## Rebase notes (against current `main`)

- Upstream relocated `isSlackPluginAccountConfigured` from `shared.ts` to a new
  `account-configured.ts`; the `trusted-upstream` branch was added there and
  `shared.ts` re-exports as before.
- `bundled-channel-config-metadata.generated.ts` was regenerated (not
  hand-merged) via `config:channels:gen`.
- The schema-regression test's negative assertion was updated to validate the
  malformed values directly against the generated Slack JSON schema (using
  `validateJsonSchemaValue`, the same helper the runtime uses), instead of the
  registry-dependent `validateConfigObjectWithPlugins` path — current `main`
  only applies channel-config patterns when the plugin schema is loaded from a
  live registry, which isn't present in a lightweight unit test.

## History

Issue #82406 ("Support trusted reverse-proxy mode for Slack gateway ingress")
was filed and then self-closed by the author pending fork validation. A
maintainer locked it as "resolved" on close, so it can no longer host
discussion. Validation is now complete and the proposal was re-filed as #91242
(open), anchored in the `trusted-proxy` precedent and the
anti-`skipSignatureVerification` framing. This PR should reference `Implements
#91242`.

target.title: fix(state): avoid sqlite wal on nfs state volumes
target.body:
## Summary

- Detect SQLite database paths on NFS-backed volumes and use rollback journaling instead of WAL.
- Keep existing WAL behavior unchanged for local filesystems; detection uses Linux `statfs` magic first, then mount metadata (`/proc/self/mountinfo` or `mount` output) for platform-specific filesystem type names.
- Pass the memory-core database path into the shared SQLite journal helper and add regression coverage for the helper plus shared state opener.

Fixes #90491

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** OpenClaw no longer forces SQLite WAL for state databases whose backing volume is reported as NFS, avoiding the WAL-on-NFS path that can corrupt persisted state during Azure Container Apps revision replacement.
- **Real environment tested:** GitHub Actions Ubuntu `24.04` runner with a real localhost NFS export mounted at `/mnt/openclaw-nfs-proof`, Node `v24.16.0`, pnpm `11.2.2`, and the production `openOpenClawStateDatabase()` opener from this branch. The proof run is https://github.com/849261680/openclaw/actions/runs/27105565187.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  - In the proof workflow, install `nfs-kernel-server` and `nfs-common`, export `/tmp/openclaw-nfs-export`, mount it as `127.0.0.1:/tmp/openclaw-nfs-export` at `/mnt/openclaw-nfs-proof`, and verify the mount with `mount`, `findmnt`, and `stat -f`.

target.title: fix(feishu): use union_id as fallback for @mention detection in multi-bot groups
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #40768

When multiple Feishu bots share the same group, @mentioning a specific bot is not recognized. The bot logs `message in group ... did not mention bot` even though it's correctly mentioned.

## Root Cause

Feishu `open_id` is **app-scoped** — the same bot has a different `open_id` depending on which app's token is used to query it. Confirmed by querying the same message with two different bot tokens:

```
# Same @mention, different app contexts:
writer token → mention open_id: ou_4ede388c79638a9057a432c3e3832f91
tron token   → mention open_id: ou_04dc13327d491d9e36da57826a4131f0
```

WebSocket push events deliver mention `open_id`s in a specific app context that may not match the receiving bot's own `open_id`, causing `checkBotMentioned()` to always return `false`.

## Fix

`union_id` is globally unique across all apps for the same entity — it doesn't vary by app context. This PR:

1. Fetches `union_id` alongside `open_id` from `bot/v3/info` at startup
2. Stores it in a new `botUnionIds` map in monitor state
3. Passes it through to `checkBotMentioned()` as a fallback
4. Matches mentions by `union_id` when `open_id` doesn't match

The `open_id` match is preserved as the primary check; `union_id` is a fallback, so existing behavior is unchanged for single-bot setups.

## Files Changed

- `types.ts`: add `botUnionId` to `FeishuProbeResult`
- `probe.ts`: extract `union_id` from `bot/v3/info` response
- `monitor.startup.ts`: propagate `botUnionId` through `FeishuMonitorBotIdentity`
- `monitor.state.ts`: add `botUnionIds` map with proper clear/delete
- `monitor.account.ts`: store/clear `botUnionId`, pass to `handleFeishuMessage`
- `bot.ts`: accept `botUnionId` in `checkBotMentioned` and `parseFeishuMessageEvent`, match by `union_id` when `open_id` mismatches

target.title: [AI-assisted] Persist agent-run error replies in chat history
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: A started Control UI agent run could surface a terminal provider/runtime error live, but `chat.history` after refresh did not retain an assistant error turn for that failed run.
- Solution: Treat agent-run failure replies as real error payloads and make Gateway persist exactly one display-safe assistant error transcript entry for returned agent errors, even when the runtime emitted only an internal error stub.
- What changed: Runner failure payloads are marked `isError`, Gateway appends a deduped `<runId>:assistant-error` transcript entry for returned agent errors, and regression coverage now locks the returned-error, runtime-stub, and duplicate/idempotency paths.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No provider behavior changes, no delivery routing changes, no config/lifecycle changes, no raw transcript writes, and no dependency or lockfile changes.

## Motivation

- A user should not lose the visible assistant error after refreshing the Control UI. If a started run fails before normal assistant output, the live error event and durable history should agree.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #89197
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: A real Gateway/WebSocket Control UI run that fails at provider transport now leaves exactly one assistant error in refreshed `chat.history`.
- Real environment tested: Disposable Linux OpenClaw checkout with the patched PR branch, real Gateway server startup, real WebSocket RPC client, channels skipped with `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`, and an intentionally unreachable OpenAI-compatible base URL to trigger the provider failure without secrets.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts -- -t 'assistant error|returned agent-run error payloads|runtime emitted an internal error stub'
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.error-broadcast.test.ts src/agents/session-tool-result-guard.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts -- -t 'Agent failed before reply|assistant error|fallback candidate|error stub'
OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 node --import tsx ../real-chat-history-proof.ts after-gateway-force-v3
/usr/bin/time -v node_modules/.bin/tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json
```

- Evidence after fix:

```text
Real runtime proof:
- provider transport failed with fetch failed / Connection error
- agent.wait status: error
- immediateHistoryCount: 3
- refreshHistoryCount: 3
- immediateAssistantCount: 1
- refreshAssistantCount: 1
- chatErrorEventCount: 1
- result: ONE_ASSISTANT_ERROR_IN_REFRESHED_HISTORY

Focused Gateway proof: 3 passed, 102 skipped.
Adjacent Gateway proof: 107 passed.
Session tool result guard proof: 35 passed.
Runner/Followup focused proof: 8 passed, 214 skipped.
git diff --check: clean.
```

- Observed result after fix: The live provider failure still broadcasts the expected chat error, and both immediate and post-reconnect `chat.history` contain one assistant error text: `Agent failed before reply: LLM request failed: network connection error.`
- What was not tested: Full repository-wide test suite, successful `tsgo` core on this testserver, real provider credentials, and a browser screenshot.
- Before evidence:

```text
Unpatched main proof for the same real Gateway/WebSocket/provider-failure path:
- sendStatus: started
- agent.wait status: ok
- immediateHistoryCount: 2
- refreshHistoryCount: 2
- immediateAssistantCount: 0
- refreshAssistantCount: 0
- chatErrorEventCount: 0
- result: ASSISTANT_ERROR_HISTORY_MISMATCH
```

## Root Cause

- Root cause: Runner final failure replies were not consistently marked as error payloads, and Gateway treated the runtime assistant-error callback as if it guaranteed a Control UI-visible `chat.history` assistant turn.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Previous coverage asserted live error broadcast behavior but did not prove refreshed `chat.history` retained an assistant error after a started real runtime failure.
- Contributing context (if known): Runtime/session guard error stubs can be internal transcript-only markers; Gateway still needs to durably mirror returned terminal agent errors into the Control UI history path.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [x] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts`, and the temporary real Gateway/WebSocket proof harness.
- Scenario the test should lock in: A started run returns an agent error; Gateway appends one assistant error transcript entry, keeps the live error terminal event, survives reconnect/history refresh, and does not duplicate the assistant error.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The source tests cover the exact Gateway and runner seams, while the real proof exercises server startup, WebSocket RPC, provider transport failure, `agent.wait`, and refreshed `chat.history` in one flow.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing broadcast tests covered visible error delivery but not durable assistant transcript history.
- If no new test is added, why not: New regression assertions were added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Control UI users should now see the same failed assistant turn after refresh that they saw live when a started agent run fails before normal assistant output.

## Diagram

```text
real provider/runtime failure
  -> runner returns final failure payload marked isError
  -> Gateway returnedAgentErrorPayloads branch
  -> append one sanitized assistant error transcript entry with <runId>:assistant-error
  -> broadcast existing chat error terminal event
  -> reconnect chat.history still includes one assistant error
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No.
- New/changed network calls? No.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No.
- Data access scope changed? No new scope; the change writes a sanitized assistant error to the same session transcript path already used for Gateway assistant persistence.
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Not applicable.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux disposable test checkout
- Runtime/container: OpenClaw repository scripts with installed dependencies
- Model/provider: OpenAI-compatible provider configured to an unreachable local base URL for deterministic transport failure; no real credentials used
- Integration/channel (if any): Control UI-style Gateway WebSocket RPC; external channels skipped with `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`
- Relevant config (redacted): Dummy redacted API key, local unreachable base URL, temporary state directory

### Steps

1. Start the real Gateway server from the patched checkout.
2. Connect a WebSocket client and send `chat.send` for a Control UI session.
3. Trigger a deterministic provider transport failure through the unreachable model base URL.
4. Wait for the run with `agent.wait`.
5. Read `chat.history` immediately.
6. Reconnect a fresh WebSocket client and read `chat.history` again.
7. Count assistant text messages and chat error events.

### Expected

- The run surfaces a live terminal error.
- Refreshed `chat.history` contains exactly one assistant error message for the failed run.
- The assistant error is not duplicated.

### Actual

- Before: refreshed `chat.history` had zero assistant messages for the failed run.
- After: refreshed `chat.history` had exactly one assistant error message and one chat error event.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

```text
Before real proof result: ASSISTANT_ERROR_HISTORY_MISMATCH
After real proof result: ONE_ASSISTANT_ERROR_IN_REFRESHED_HISTORY
After source proof: Gateway focused 3/3, Gateway adjacent 107/107, session guard 35/35, Runner/Followup focused 8/8.
TypeScript core check on the testserver did not produce diagnostics; the process was terminated by signal 9 at about 1.78 GB RSS, so this is reported as a testserver resource blocker rather than a green typecheck claim.
```

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios: Returned agent-run error payload detection, runner final failure `isError` marking, Gateway assistant-error transcript append, runtime internal error-stub callback interaction, idempotency key behavior, real provider transport failure, immediate history, refreshed history after reconnect, and existing chat error event behavior.
- Edge cases checked: Runtime internal error stub does not suppress the Control UI-visible returned error transcript; Gateway writes one `<runId>:assistant-error` entry for the returned terminal error; no dependency or lockfile files changed.
- What you did **not** verify: Full repo-wide suite, successful `tsgo` core on the resource-limited testserver, real external provider credentials, screenshot/recording, and production channels.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes.
- Config/env changes? No.
- Migration needed? No.
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: Not applicable.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: A runtime-internal error stub could suppress the Control UI-visible returned error transcript.
  - Mitigation: Returned terminal agent errors now force the Gateway assistant-error transcript append while retaining the existing idempotency key.
- Risk: Gateway could duplicate assistant errors.
  - Mitigation: The transcript append uses a stable `<runId>:assistant-error` idempotency key and the source tests cover the one-message behavior.
- Risk: Error text could bypass display sanitization.
  - Mitigation: The Gateway fallback still uses `sanitizeAssistantDisplayText` before transcript append.
- Risk: Dependency Guard could require security approval.
  - Mitigation: No dependency manifests, lockfiles, or dependency blocks were changed.

- add focused tests covering request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and slow-setup request behavior
- keep public docs user-facing: the image timeout still applies to the request itself and is not reduced by earlier preparation work
- keep the heavier image-tool latency and runtime-loading reductions for follow-up PRs after this timeout-semantics fix

target.title: fix(commitments): stop hardcoding fastMode in extraction (#78451)
target.body:
## Summary

MiniMax Coding Plan operators whose plan owns `MiniMax-M2.7` but not the `-highspeed` variant hit `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` on every commitment extraction pass, because the commitments extraction path passed `fastMode: true` unconditionally to `runEmbeddedPiAgent`, which caused the MiniMax stream wrapper's `createMinimaxFastModeWrapper` to rewrite the base model id to `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`.

## Root Cause

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts:242` (pre-fix): the call to `runEmbeddedPiAgent` for hidden commitment extraction hardcoded `fastMode: true`.
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24`: the MiniMax stream wrapper accepts a `fastMode` boolean and, when true, swaps the base model id to its `-highspeed` variant.
- Result: every commitments extraction request silently flipped `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, which Coding Plan operators do not own. MiniMax responds with HTTP 500 / error code 2061. Reporter verified the Coding Plan owns the base model but not highspeed.
- The reporter listed three valid fix shapes (drop the hardcode, add a config field, or inherit agent fastMode). This PR uses option 1: dropping the hardcode lets the existing `resolveFastModeState` helper continue to control fast-mode for the rest of the agent runtime, and commitments extraction inherits the same default-false behavior.

## Changes

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts`: remove `fastMode: true` from the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload built in the commitments extraction path. Add a comment explaining the reason so this does not regress.
- `src/commitments/runtime.test.ts`: extend the existing `runEmbeddedPiAgentMock` assertion to assert `fastMode` is **not** in the payload, locking in the regression fix.
- `CHANGELOG.md`: single-line entry under Unreleased / Fixes.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: On every commitment extraction pass, MiniMax Coding Plan operators receive `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` from the MiniMax server because the agent runtime requested `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed` even though their plan only owns `MiniMax-M2.7`.
